title: Chapter 24
text: 3 Bifurcations 3.1 Saddle-Node Bifurcation 3.1.1 x ˙   =   1   +   r x   +   x 2 The fixed points are x   =   − r   ±   r 2   −   4 2 2   fixed   points   exist   | r |   >   2 1   fixed   point   exists   if   r   =   ± 2 0   fixed   points   exist   if   | r |   <   2 ⇒   Saddle-node   bifurcation   occurs   at   r   =   ± 2 To sketch the vector fields for different values of r , draw y = 1 + x 2 and y = − rx . The intersections represent the fixed points, and the flow is to the right and left if 1 + x 2 > − rx and 1 + x 2 < − rx respectively. The bifurcation diagram is x   =   − r   ±   r 2   −   4 2 or equivalently r   =   − ( x   +   1 x ) , which is easier to plot. 3.1.3 x ˙   =   r   +   x   −   ln ( 1   +   x ) To sketch the vector fields for different values of r , draw y = r + x and y = ln(1 + x ). The intersections represent the fixed points, and the flow is to the right and left if r + x > ln(1 + x ) and r + x < ln(1 + x ) respectively. The bifurcation occurs when the curves y = r + x and y = ln(1 + x ) have a tangential intersection. We can find this r value analytically by solving the equations r   +   x   =   ln ( 1   +   x )   and   d d x   ( r   +   x )   =   1   =   1 1   +   x   =   d d x   ( ln ( 1   +   ) ) 1   =   1 1   +   x   ⇒   x   =   0   r   +   x   =   ln ( 1   +   x )   →   r   =   ( 1 )   =   0 Hence, a saddle-node bifurcation occurs at r = 0. 3.1.5 a) x ˙   =   r 2   −   x 2 A saddle-node bifurcation doesn't occur. Instead there is more of a transcritical bifurcation, but not quite since both of the fixed-point positions change with r . b) x ˙   =   r 2   +   x 2 Again, a saddle-node bifurcation doesn’t occur. In this case there is almost nothing to plot on the bifurcation diagram. 3.2 Transcritical Bifurcation 3.2.1 x ˙   =   r x   +   x 2 Transcritical bifurcation occurs at r = 0. 3.2.3 x ˙   =   x   −   r x ( 1   −   x ) Note: The positive fixed point goes to x = ∞ as r → 0 − , disappears when r = 0, then reappears at x = −∞ when r becomes slightly positive. Transcritical bifurcation occurs at r = 1. 3.2.5 a) A   +   X   ⇌ k − 1 k 1   2 X X   +   B   → k 2   C The k 1 reaction has a net gain of one X at rate k 1 [ A ][ X ], the k _1 reaction has a net loss of one X at rate k _1 [ X ][ X ] = k _1 [ X ] 2 , and the k 2 reaction has a net loss of one X at rate k 2 [ B ][ X ], making the rate of concentration change [ X ˙ ]   =   x ˙ the sum of the rates for positive gains and negative losses. x ˙ =   k 1 a x   −   k − 1   x 2   −   k 2 b x =   ( k 1 a   −   k 2 b ) x   −   k − 1 x 2 =   c 1   −   c 2 x 2 So c 1 = k 1 a – k 2 b and c 2 = k _1 . b) d x ˙ d x =   k 1 a   −   k 2 b   −   2 k − 1 x d x ˙ d x | x * = 0 =   k 1 a   −   k 1 a   −   k 2 b   <   0 ⇒   k 1 a   <   k 2 b This makes sense chemically because the amount of chemical X will always either increase or decrease to 0. Chemicals A and B are considered inexhaustible, so if the first reaction creates X faster than the second reaction irreversibly destroys X ( k 2 b < k 1 a ), then the amount of X will grow unbounded. But if the first reaction creates X slower than the second reaction irreversibly destroys X ( k 1 a < k 2 b ), then the amount of X will approach 0. 3.3 Laser Threshold 3.3.1 n ˙ =   G n N   −   k n N ˙ =   − G n N   −   f N   +   p a) If N ˙   ≈   0 , then 0 ≈   − G n N   −   f N   +   p N ≈   p G n   +   f n ˙ =   G n N   −   k n   ≈   G p n G n   +   f   −   k n b) d n ˙ d n =   G p f ( G n   +   f ) 2   −   k d n ˙ d n | n * = 0 =   G p f f 2   −   k   =   G p f   −   k   >   0 ⇒   p   >   k f G p   =   p c c) If we set G = k = f = 1, n ˙   ≈   p m n   +   1   −   n   =   0   ⇒   n   =   0   or   n   =   p   −   1 A transcritical bifurcation occurs at p c . d) n ˙   =   G n N   −   k n   =   ( G N   −   k ) n N ˙   =   − G n N   −   f N   +   p   =   − ( G n   +   f )   N   +   p The assumption that N almost isn’t changing relative to n is valid if | GN – k | ≪ | Gn + f )|. Then N will initially change far more rapidly than n , but then stay put once it reaches an equilibrium with the current n value. Now n will still change, but N will reach the equilibrium with the newer n value almost instantly, keeping N ˙   ≈   0 . 3.4 Pitchfork Bifurcation 3.4.1 x ˙   =   r x   +   4 x 3 A subcritical pitchfork bifurcation occurs at r = 0. 3.4.3 x ˙   =   r x   −   4 x 3 A supercritical pitchfork bifurcation occurs at r = 0. 3.4.5 x ˙   =   r   −   3 x 2 A saddle-node bifurcation occurs at r = 0. 3.4.7 x ˙   =   5   −   r e − x 2 A saddle-node bifurcation occurs at r = 5. 3.4.9 x ˙   =   x   +   tanh ( r x ) A subcritical pitchfork bifurcation occurs at r = −1. 3.4.11 x ˙   =   r x   −   sin ( x ) a) There are an infinite number of fixed points when r = 0 at every integer multiple of π. All even multiples of π are stable, and all odd multiples of π are unstable. b) For r ≥ 1, there is only one intersection between y = rx and y = sin( x ), which is an unstable fixed point at the origin. c) There are no bifurcations until r ≤ 1, and the first bifurcation is a subcritical pitchfork bifurcation at r = 1. Note: To add more rigor to our bifurcation classification, we can expand in a Taylor series about x = 0, which gives x ˙   =   r x   −   sin ( x )   ≈   r x   −   ( x   −   x 3 3 !   +   x 5 5 !   −   ⋯ )   ≈   ( r   −   1 ) x   +   x 3 3 ! This is the normal form of a subcritical pitchfork bifurcation at r – 1 = 0. There are more bifurcations as r continues to decrease toward zero. However, all these subsequent bifurcations are saddle-node bifurcations. The first of these bifurcations occurs at r ≈ 0.1284 when the line y = rx becomes tangent to a hilltop of the sine wave. Saddle-node bifurcations occur repeatedly as the line y = rx becomes tangent to the next hilltop of the sine wave, and an infinite number of hilltops implies an infinite number of saddle-node bifurcations. d) To find a formula for the r values of the saddle-node bifurcations, we can use the fact that y = rx and y = sin( x ) tangentially intersect at the bifurcation values. r x   =   sin ( x )   d d x ( r x )   =   r   =   cos ( x )   =   d d x ( sin ( x ) )   ⇒   x   =   tan ( x )   ⇒   r   =   cos ( x ) Therefore, the saddle-node bifurcation values are r = cos( x *) where x * is a root of the equation x * = tan( x *). For an approximate formula when 0 < r ≪ 1, the saddle-node bifurcations occur approximately at the hilltops of the sine wave, since y = rx intersects y = sin( x ) tangentially and y = rx is almost flat. Therefore, the bifurcations on the positive x -axis and negative x -axis respectively occur approximately at the local minimums and local maximums of sin( x ). These points are { ± ( 4 n   +   1 ) π 2   :   n   ∈   ℕ } We have to skip the first local minimum and local maximum on the positive x-axis and negative x-axis respectively because they are involved in the subcritical pitchfork bifurcation. The approximate values of r at which the saddle-node bifurcations occur are the reciprocals of that set. r   ∈   { ± 2 ( 4 n   +   1 ) π   :   n   ∈   ℕ } So x and r in the first and second set respectively implies rx = ±1 and sin( x ) = ±1, which will cancel out in ẋ = rx – sin( x ). e) For −∞ < r < 0, we again have saddle-node bifurcations occurring. But there is no accompanying pitchfork bifurcation because after r < −0.2172 there is only one fixed point left at x = 0. f) The bifurcation diagram is made of the line x = 0 and the curve r   =   sin ( x ) x plotted sideways. Hence, x can take on any value from positive to negative infinity. 3.4.13 a) x ˙   =   r   −   x   −   e − x b) x ˙   =   1   −   x   −   e − r x 3.4.15 x ˙   =   r x   +   x 3   −   x 5   =   −   d V d x   ⇒   V   =   − r x 2 2   −   x 4 4   +   x 6 6   +   C The critical points occur when d V d x =   − r x   −   x 3   +   x 5 =   − x ( r   +   x 2   −   x 4 )   =   0 x 1 =   0 x 2 =   − 1   ±   1 2   −   4 ( − 1 ) r 2 ( − 1 )   =   1   ±   1   +   4 r 2 x 2 =   1   +   1   −   4 x 2   x 3   =   1   −   1   +   4 r 2 x 4 =   −   1   +   1   +   4 r 2   x 5   =   − 1   −   1   +   4 r 2 and the local minima occur when d 2 V d x 2   =   − r   − 3 x 2   +   5 x 4   >   0 d 2 V d x 2   ( x 1 )   =   − r   >   0   if   r   <   0 d 2 V d x 2   ( x 2 )   =   d 2 V d x 2   ( x 4 )   =   4 r   +   4 r   +   1   +   1   >   0   if   − 1 4   <   r d 2 V d x 2   ( x 3 )   =   d 2 V d x 2   ( x 5 )   =   4 r   −   4 r   +   1   +   1   >   0   if   0   <   r so the three candidate wells occur at x 1 , x 2 , and x 4 with − 1 4   <   r   <   0 . V ( x 0 ) = C , so for simplicity we’ll set C = 0, which leaves           V ( x 2 )   =   V ( x 4 )   =   V ( x 0 ) 1 24   ( − ( 4 r   +   1 ) 3 2   −   6 r   −   1 )   =   0   ⇒   r   =   − 3 16 So r c   =   − 3 16 and the three minima x 1 , x 2 , and x 4 occur at the value of the integrating constant C . 3.5 Overdamped Bead on a Rotating Hoop 3.5.1 There shouldn’t be an equilibrium between π 2 and π because the rotation would force the bead outward and down, and gravity would force the bead down. The forces wouldn’t cancel, so the bead would move. 3.5.3 A Taylor series expansion of f ( ϕ ) = sin( ϕ )(γ cos( ϕ ) – 1) centered at ϕ = 0 gives ( γ   −   1 ) ϕ   +   ( 1 6   −   2 γ 3 )   ϕ 3   +   O ( ϕ 5 ) 3.5.5 a) Starting with the dimensionless equation ϵ   d 2 ϕ d τ 2   +   d ϕ d τ   =   f ( ϕ ) We already have T slow   =   t τ   =   b m g from the text, but we don’t know what order of magnitude T faster is in relation to T slow , so we’ll guess a new time scale τ = ϵ k ξ . The dimensionless equation is now ϵ 1 − 2 k   d 2 ϕ d ξ 2   +   ϵ − k   d ϕ d ξ   =   f ( ϕ ) We still need to be able to solve the equation, meaning each of the terms must be of the same order or negligible compared to all the other terms. To have all the terms of the same order, we need ϵ 1–2 k = ϵ − k = 1, which is impossible. To have two terms of the same order and one term negligible, we need ϵ 1 − 2 k   =   ϵ − k   ≫   1   ⇒   k   =   1   or   ϵ 1 − 2 k   =   1   ≫   ϵ − k   ⇒   k   =   1 2   or   ϵ − k   =   1   ≫   ϵ 1 − 2 k   ⇒   k   =   0 Choosing k = 0 gives the equation we started with, and k   =   1 2 gives d 2 ϕ d ξ 2   +   1 ϵ   d ϕ d ξ   =   f ( ϕ ) which is bad because ϵ ≪ 1 makes the 1 ϵ term enormous. But the k = 1 choice looks good. If we choose τ = ϵξ for the new time scale, then we can find T fast in terms of m, g, r, ω , and b by using T slow   =   t τ   =   b m g from the text. T fast   =   t ξ   =   t ξ   ( τ t   b m g )   =   τ ξ   b m g   =   ϵ b m g   =   m 2 g r b 2   b m g   =   m r b b) Rescaling with the fast time scale r g T fast 2   d 2 ϕ d ξ 2 =   − b m g T fast   d ϕ d ξ   −   sin ( ϕ )   +   τ ω 2 g   sin ( ϕ )   cos ( ϕ ) b 2 m 2   g r   d 2 ϕ d τ 2 =   − b m 2 g r   d ϕ d τ   −   sin ( ϕ )   +   τ ω 2 g   sin ( ϕ )   cos ( ϕ ) b 2 m 2   g r   d 2 ϕ d τ 2 +   b 2 m 2 g r   d ϕ d τ   =   sin ( ϕ )   +   τ ω 2 g   sin ( ϕ )   cos ( ϕ ) d 2 ϕ d τ 2   +   d ϕ d τ =   m 2   g r b 2   ( −   sin ( ϕ )   +   τ ω 2 g   sin ( ϕ )   cos ( ϕ ) ) =   ϵ   f ( ϕ ) Now the ϵf ( ϕ ) is the negligible term. c) T fast   =   ϵ   b m g   =   ϵ T slow So if ϵ ≪ 1 then T fast ≪ T slow . 3.5.7 N ˙   =   r N   ( 1   −   N K )   N ( 0 )   =   N 0 a) r   ~   1 time   K   ~   population       N 0   ~   population b) N ˙   =   r N ( 1   −   N K )   N ( 0 )   =   N 0 x   =   N K K x ˙   =   r K x ( 1   −   x )   x ( 0 )   =   N 0 K   =   x 0 x ˙   =   r x ( 1   −   x )   x ( 0 )   =   x 0 τ   =   r t r   d x d τ   =   r x ( 1   −   x )   x ( 0 )   =   x 0 d x d τ   =   x ( 1   −   x )   x ( 0 )   =   x 0 c) N ˙   =   r N ( 1   −   N K )   N ( 0 )   =   N 0 u   =   N N 0 N 01 u ˙   =   r N 0 u   ( 1   −   N 0 K   u )   u ( 0 )   =   N ( 0 ) N 0   =   1 1 r   u ˙   =   u ( 1   −   N 0 K   u ) τ   =   r t d u d τ   =   u ( 1   −   N 0 K   u ) k   =   r t d u d τ   =   u ( 1   −   k u )   u ( 0 )   =   1 d) The x nondimensionalization would be useful for investigating the effect of the initial condition relative to carrying capacity on the solution, whereas the u nondimensionalization would be useful for studying the rate of population growth relative to carrying capacity. By the way, scaling in such a way that all solutions have the same initial condition is mathematically valid but runs counter to the geometric approach we are using in this book. Our whole approach is based on visualizing different solutions emanating from different initial conditions. 3.6 Imperfect Bifurcations and Catastrophes 3.6.1 x ˙   =   h   +   r x   −   x 3 This x versus r bifurcation diagram has a saddle-node bifurcation at a negative x value. Vertically shifting the cubic equation and then varying r will achieve this. So h is positive. 3.6.3 x ˙   =   r x   +   a x 2   −   x 3 a) b) 3.6.5 a) The spring has spring constant k and rest length L 0 , but only the force from the spring that is parallel, and not perpendicular, with the wire will affect the bead. k ( L   −   L 0 ) =   k ( x 2   +   a 2   −   L 0 )   x x 2   +   a 2 =   − k x ( 1   −   L 0 x 2   +   a 2 ) The force of gravity will also move the bead along the wire, but only the fraction of the force that is parallel with the wire. m g   sin ( θ ) At equilibrium, the force of the spring and the force from gravity have to be equal and opposite, which gives m g   sin ( θ )   =   k x ( 1   −   L 0 x 2   +   a 2 ) b) m g   sin ( θ )   =   k x   ( 1   −   L 0 x 2   +   a 2 ) m g   sin ( θ )   =   k x   −   k x   L 0 x 2   +   a 2 m g   sin ( θ )   −   k x   =   − k x L 0 a x 2 a 2   +   1 − m g   sin ( θ ) k x   +   1   =   L 0 a x 2 a 2   +   1 1   −   m g   sin ( θ ) k x   =   L 0 a +   1 x 2 a 2 u   =   x a   R   =   L 0 a   h   =   m g   sin ( θ ) k a 1   −   h u   =   R 1   +   u 2 c) Both h and R are strictly positive quantities from the physical constants they’re made of. The following graphs show the different types and number of possible intersections. In each graph, h shapes the discontinuous graph and R shapes the bell-shaped graph with u as the independent variable. For 0 < R < 1 there is only one intersection since the other curve asymptotes to height 1 from above. As R increases, there is a point when the two curves just touch, making another fixed point in a saddle-node bifurcation. As R increases further there are three fixed points. d) 1   −   h u   =   R 1   +   u 2 r   =   R   −   1 1   −   h u   =   r   +   1 1   +   u 2 u 1   +   u 2   −   h 1   +   u 2   =   ( r   +   1 ) u h 1   +   u 2   +   ( r   +   1 ) u   −   u 1   +   u 2   =   0 Taylor series expansion h ( 1   +   1 2 u 2   +   O ( u 4 ) )   +   ( r   +   1 ) u   −   u ( 1   +   1 2 u 2   +   O ( u 4 ) )   =   0 h   +   h 2 u 2   +   r u   +   u   −   u   −   1 2 u 3   =   O ( u 4 ) h   +   r u   +   h 2 u 2   −   1 2 u 3   =   O ( u 4 ) h   +   r u   +   h 2 u 2   −   1 2 u 3   ≈   0 e) The saddle-node bifurcations will occur approximately at the local maximum of the approximate equation when the slopes of these equations are equal at an equal height. 1 2 u 3   −   r u   =   h   +   h 2 u 2 1 2 u 3   −   r u   =   h   +   h 2 u 2 d d u   ( 1 2 u 3   −   r u ) =   3 2 u 2   −   r   =   h u   =   d d u   ( h   +   h 2 u 2 ) 3 2 u 2   −   h u   =   r 1 2 u 3   −   r u   =   1 2 u 3   −   ( 3 2 u 2   −   h u )   u =   h   +   h 2 u 2 1 2 u 3   −   3 2 u 3   +   h u 2 =   h   +   h 2 u 2 h 2 u 2   −   h =   u 3 h ( 1 2 u 2   −   1 ) =   u 3 h =   2 u 3 u 2   −   2 r   =   3 2 u 2   −   h u =   3 2 u 2   −   2 u 3 u 2   −   2   u   =   u 4   +   3 u 2 2 ( 1   −   u 2 ) f) Here we use the same procedure as in part (e). The saddle-node bifurcations will occur at the local maximum of the equation when the slopes of these equations are equal at an equal height. 1     −   h u   =   R 1   +   u 2 d d u   ( 1   −   h u )   =   h u 2 =   − R u ( 1   +   u 2 ) 3 2   =   d d u   ( R 1   +   u 2 ) h ( 1   +   u 2 ) 3 2 =   − R u 3 R =   − h ( 1   +   u 2 ) 3 2 u 3 1   −   h u =   R 1   +   u 2   =   − h ( 1   +   u 2 ) u 3 1   +   u 2   =   − h ( 1   +   u 2 ) u 3 u 3   −   h u 2 =   − h ( 1   +   u 2 ) h =   − u 3 R =   − h ( 1   +   u 2 ) 3 2 u 3   =   − ( − u 3 )   ( 1   +   u 2 ) 3 2 u 3   =   ( 1   +   u 2 ) 3 2 The approximate result and the exact result agree in the limit of u → 0. h   =   u 3   ≈   2 u 3 0   −   2   ≈   2 u 3 u 2   −   2 R   =   ( 1   +   u 2 ) 3 2   ≈   ( 1   +   0 2 ) 3 2   ≈   0 4   +   3 ( 0 ) 2 2 ( 1   −   0 2 )   +   1   ≈   u 4   +   3 u 2 2 ( 1   −   u 2 )   +   1   =   r   +   1 g) h ( u )   =   − u 3   R ( u )   =   ( 1   +   u 2 ) 3 2   ⇒   r ( u )   =   ( 1   +   u 2 ) 3 2   −   1 h) h ( u )   =   − u 3 R ( u )   =   ( 1   +   u 2 ) 3 2 u   =   x a h   =   − ( x a ) 3 =   m g   sin ( θ ) k a   R   =   ( 1   +   ( x a ) 2 ) 3 2   =   L 0 a Solving for x and equating the two equations gives the conditions that the catastrophe will occur and the position x of its occurrence. Since many of the parameters are fixed, we could find the catastrophe angle θ in terms of the physical constants of the experiment. 3.6.7 a) h   =   T arctanh ( m )   −   J n m We can nondimensionalize to α   +   β m   =   arctanh ( m )   α   =   h T   β   =   J n T   >   0 There is always a unique intersection point with this range of β . Now there are one, two, or three solutions depending on the values depending on α for this range of β . b) If h = 0 then α = 0 and the phase transition occurs when the slope of the line β   =   J n T c   =   1   ⇒   T c   =   J n . 3.7 Insect Outbreak 3.7.1 d x d τ   =   r x ( 1   −   x k )   −   x 2 1   +   x 2 d d x   ( r x ( 1   −   x k )   −   x 2 1   +   x 2 )   =   − 2 r x k   +   r   −   2 x ( x 2   +   1 ) 2 d d x   ( r x ( 1   −   x k )   −   x 2 1   +   x 2 ) | x = 0   =   r   >   0 So x = 0 is always unstable. 3.7.3 N ˙   =   r N ( 1   −   N K )   −   H a) N ˙ r K   =   N K   ( 1   −   N K )   −   H r H x   =   N K   τ   =   r t   h   =   H r K d x d τ   =   x ( 1   −   x )   −   h b) c) A saddle-node bifurcation occurs at h c   =   1 4 and x   =   1 2 . d) For h < h c the fish are sustainably harvested so that their population never decreases or increases. For h c < h the fish are hunted to extinction (assuming that the fish population can’t become negative). 3.7.5 a) g ˙   =   d g d t   =   k 1 s 0   −   k 2 g   +   k 3 g 2 k 4 2   +   g 2 k 4 k 3   d g k 4 d t   =   k 1 s 0 k 3   −   k 2 k 4 k 3   g k 4   +   g 2 k 4 2 1   +   g 2 k 4 2 x   =   g k 4   s   =   k 1 s 0 k 3   r   =   k 2 k 4 k 3   τ   =   k 3 k 4   t d x d τ   =   s   −   r x   +   x 2 1   +   x 2 b) If s = 0 d x d τ   =   0   −   r x   +   x 2 1   +   x 2   =   0 x   =   0 ,   1   ±   1   −   4 r 2 2 r And there are three real fixed points, two of which are positive since the square root is at most one, if r   <   r c   =   1 2 . c) g (0) = 0 corresponds to x (0) = 0, and increasing the parameter s translates the graph vertically. There is only one fixed point at x = 0 for 1 2   <   r since the graph is strictly decreasing, and x will stay close to the fixed point as s is increased. At r   ≤   1 2 one, two, and three fixed points are possible with two saddle-node bifurcations occurring as s increases. Starting with s = 0, increasing s , and then returning to s = 0: If 1 2   <   r , the system will go towards the x = 0 stable fixed point since that is the only fixed point. If r   =   1 2 , the system can go back to the x = 0 fixed point or the stable half of the semistable fixed point depending on how fast s changes, but noise in a real system would most likely bump x to the unstable side of the semistable fixed point and then the system would go towards the x = 0 fixed point. If r   <   1 2 , the system will almost surely be near the far right stable fixed point, despite s returning to its original value. d) The saddle-node bifurcations will occur when d x d τ   =   0 and d d x   d x d τ   =   0 , which gives us two equations to solve for r and s in terms of x . d d x   ( s   −   r x   +   x 2 1   +   x 2 )   =   − r   +   2 x ( 1   +   x 2 ) 2   =   0 r   =   2 x ( 1   +   x 2 ) 2 s   −   r x   +   x 2 1   +   x 2   =   s   −   2 x ( 1   +   x 2 ) 2 x   +   x 2 1   +   x 2   =   0 s   =   x 2 ( 1   −   x 2 ) ( x 2   +   1 ) 2 These are saddle-node bifurcations. e) 3.7.7 a) lim n → ∞   β p n K n   +   p n   =   lim n → ∞   β p n p n K n p n   +   p n p n   =   lim n → ∞   β K n p n   +   1   =   β H   ( p   −   K ) where H ( p ) is the Heaviside step function. b) c) We’ll assume α > 0 and β > 0 due to being biological measurements, as well as δK > β from the problem statement. If we start α = 0 and increase α , we’ll get a saddle-node bifurcation at p ≈ K when the line h ( p ) touches the top corner of g ( p ). Then we get another saddle-node bifurcation when h ( p ) touches the bottom corner of g ( p ). In summary, the fixed points are 0   <   α   <   δ K   −   β ⇒ p   ≈   α δ   stable α   ≈   δ K   −   β ⇒ p   ≈   α δ ,   K   stable,   semistable δ K   −   β   <   α   <   δ K ⇒ p   ≈   α δ ,   K ,   α   +   β δ   stable,   unstable,   stable α   ≈   δ K ⇒ p   ≈   K ,   α   +   β δ   semistable ,   stable δ K   <   α ⇒ p   ≈   α   +   β δ   stable and now we can plot the bifurcation diagram. d) Hysteresis is possible for this system, in the path shown through parameter space below for example. Starting at α = 0, p = 0, the system stays at the lower fixed point until α increases past δK and the system jumps to the upper fixed point. The system exhibits hysteresis because the system does not jump back to the lower fixed point once α decreases past δK , despite the lower fixed point existing. Instead, we have to decrease α past δK – β in order for the system to jump back to the lower fixed point. Here is a similar path viewed on the phase line where we have combined the graph of both functions as g ( p ) – h ( p ) with a very large n value, K = 3, δ = 1, and β = 2. The system exhibits hysteresis when p starts at the left fixed point but moves to the right fixed point once 3 = δK < α . But then α must be decreased to α < δK – β = 1 instead of α < δK = 3 for p to go back to the original left fixed point.
